petitioner_background: The petitioner is a self-represented individual seeking classification as an employment-based immigrant under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Act as an alien of extraordinary ability, described as a 'Physical Rehabilitation and Massage Therapist expert.' The record indicates he worked as a massage expert and recreation specialist for the Multinational Force and Observers from 1998 to 2005, and previously served as a massage expert 'for high diplomats' at the United States Embassy in Cairo from 1983 to 1995. He also had a background in competitive wrestling in Egyptian district championships during 1975-1977 and participated in a 1984 Sinai 10Km Race. He submitted March 2007 letters from the Shawl Injury Rehabilitation Center and Gold's Gym in Oakland, California expressing interest in employing him.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: At the time of filing, the petitioner submitted letters and certificates of appreciation from the Multinational Force and Observers complimenting him for his services to the organization. On appeal, he submitted additional certificates of appreciation. He also submitted a certificate from the Multinational Force and Observers stating that he 'successfully participated in the 1984 Sinai 10Km Race.' Additionally, he submitted a Certificate of Appreciation from the Egyptian 'Ministry of Interior, General Department for Police Sporting Union' stating that he 'participated in activities and championship of the District in wrestling (Roman & Free Style) during the period from 1975 to 1977 and he has achieved many championship of the District; he has a high physical spirit and a high technical skill.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the letters and certificates of recognition and appreciation from his employer (Multinational Force and Observers) were not established to be tantamount to nationally or internationally recognized prizes or awards for excellence in the field of endeavor. The 1984 Sinai 10Km Race certificate was simply an acknowledgment of participation rather than a nationally or internationally recognized prize or award for excellence, and the petitioner had not established that competitive running is his field of endeavor or that he has competed regularly since 1984. Regarding the Egyptian Ministry of Interior certificate, the AAO found that success in District championships reflected only regional recognition rather than national or international recognition, and the petitioner had not established that competitive wrestling is his field of endeavor or that he has competed regularly since the 1970s. The statute and regulations require sustained national or international acclaim and continued work in the area of expertise. The petitioner failed to submit sufficient documentary evidence that he received any nationally or internationally recognized prize or award related to his field of physical rehabilitation and massage.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: At the time of filing, the petitioner failed to submit any documentary evidence relating to this criterion. On appeal, the petitioner stated that he had received 'frequent media recognition through interviews for articles in governmental newspapers and magazines in Egypt,' and submitted copies of several articles. However, these were unaccompanied by certified English language translations.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO explained that to meet this criterion, published material must be primarily about the petitioner and printed in professional or major trade publications or other major media with significant national or international distribution. The articles submitted on appeal were unaccompanied by certified English language translations as required by 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3), which requires foreign language documents to be accompanied by a full English translation certified as complete and accurate by a competent translator. Further, there was no evidence showing that the articles were published in professional or major trade publications or some other form of major media. Accordingly, the petitioner did not meet this criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted letters and other supporting documentation indicating that he worked as a massage expert and recreation specialist for the Multinational Force and Observers from 1998 to 2005.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO explained that at issue are the position the petitioner was selected to fill and the reputation of the entity that selected him — the position must be of such significance that selection to fill it is indicative of or consistent with national or international acclaim. There was no supporting evidence showing that the Multinational Force and Observers has a distinguished reputation in the petitioner's field of endeavor (physical rehabilitation and massage). Further, there was no evidence indicating that the petitioner's role was leading or critical for the organization's operations. The documentation did not establish that he was responsible for the success or standing of the Multinational Force and Observers to a degree consistent with the meaning of 'leading or critical role' and indicative of sustained national or international acclaim.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO concurred with the director's finding that the petitioner failed to demonstrate receipt of a major internationally recognized award or to meet at least three of the regulatory criteria required to establish sustained national or international acclaim. The conclusion reached by considering evidence under each criterion separately was consistent with a review of the evidence in the aggregate; even in the aggregate, the evidence did not distinguish the petitioner as one of the small percentage at the very top of the field. The AAO also concurred that the petitioner failed to establish he would continue to work in his area of expertise in the United States, and found beyond the decision of the director that his occupation as a Physical Rehabilitation and Massage Therapist expert does not fall within the enumerated categories of sciences, arts, education, business, or athletics under section 203(b)(1)(A)(i). Additionally, the AAO concurred that work with clients at a rehabilitation center or gym in Oakland would provide prospective benefit so attenuated at the national level as to be negligible. The petition was denied for these reasons, each considered as an independent and alternative basis for denial.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1153(b)(1)(A)
Janka v. U.S. Dept. of Transp., NTSB, 925 F.2d 1147 (9th Cir. 1991)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: Procedural issue: Foreign language articles submitted on appeal were unaccompanied by certified English language translations as required by 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3), which mandates a full certified English translation for any foreign language document submitted to USCIS. Additionally, the petitioner was self-represented throughout the proceeding.
